questions.q: Which article lays down the procedure for amending the Constitution of India?
questions.explanation: Article 368 in Part XX prescribes the procedure for amending the Constitution.

questions.q: An amendment to the Constitution must generally be passed by which body?
questions.explanation: Constitutional amendments are initiated and passed by Parliament under Article 368.

questions.q: Most amendments under Article 368 require what kind of majority in each House?
options: A two-thirds majority of the whole country
questions.explanation: Article 368 requires a special majority in each House to pass most amendments.

questions.q: An amendment Bill can be introduced in which House or Houses of Parliament?
questions.explanation: An amendment Bill may be introduced in either House of Parliament.

questions.q: After both Houses pass an amendment, whose assent is required for it to become law?
questions.explanation: An amendment Bill requires the assent of the President to come into force.

questions.q: Roughly how many times has the Constitution been amended since 1950?
questions.explanation: The Constitution has been amended over a hundred times since it came into force in 1950.

questions.q: Certain amendments, in addition to Parliament, also require ratification by what fraction of the states?
options: At least half of the states
questions.explanation: Amendments affecting federal provisions need ratification by at least half of the state legislatures.

questions.q: Article 368 is found in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 368 is contained in Part XX of the Constitution, which deals with amendments.

questions.q: The very first amendment to the Constitution was enacted in which year?
questions.explanation: The First Amendment to the Constitution was passed in 1951.

questions.q: For an amendment Bill, is there any provision for a joint sitting to resolve a deadlock between the Houses?
options: Yes, a joint sitting is always held
No, both Houses must separately pass it
Yes, but only for money provisions
questions.explanation: There is no provision for a joint sitting for amendment Bills; both Houses must pass them separately.

questions.q: A special majority under Article 368 means a majority of the total membership of the House and how many of those present and voting?
questions.explanation: A special majority requires more than half the total membership and at least two-thirds of members present and voting.

questions.q: Amendments changing the election of the President or the distribution of powers between Union and States require ratification by:
questions.explanation: Such federal amendments need ratification by at least half of the state legislatures in addition to Parliament.

questions.q: Which amendment added the words 'socialist' and 'secular' to the Preamble and is called the 'Mini-Constitution'?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment of 1976 made wide-ranging changes and is known as the Mini-Constitution.

questions.q: Which amendment lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 years?
questions.explanation: The 61st Amendment of 1988 lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 years.

questions.q: Which amendment introduced the Anti-Defection Law through the Tenth Schedule?
questions.explanation: The 52nd Amendment of 1985 added the Tenth Schedule containing the Anti-Defection Law.

questions.q: Which amendments gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj and urban local bodies in 1992?
questions.explanation: The 73rd and 74th Amendments of 1992 gave constitutional status to rural and urban local bodies.

questions.q: Which amendment introduced the Goods and Services Tax in 2016?
questions.explanation: The 101st Amendment of 2016 introduced the Goods and Services Tax.

questions.q: Which amendment made elementary education a Fundamental Right by inserting Article 21A?
questions.explanation: The 86th Amendment of 2002 inserted Article 21A, making free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right.

questions.q: Three categories of amendment procedure exist; which type can be done by a simple majority of Parliament?
options: Amendments outside the scope of Article 368
questions.explanation: Some provisions can be changed by simple majority, a process not treated as an amendment under Article 368.

questions.q: Which amendment is often noted for reducing some emergency provisions and restoring certain safeguards after 1977?
questions.explanation: The 44th Amendment of 1978 revised several provisions, including replacing 'internal disturbance' with 'armed rebellion' for emergencies.

questions.q: Which amendment provided for the National Capital Territory of Delhi's special administrative arrangement in 1991?
questions.explanation: The 69th Amendment of 1991 gave the National Capital Territory of Delhi a Legislative Assembly.

questions.q: An amendment Bill, once passed and assented to, is numbered and titled as a:
questions.explanation: A successful amendment becomes a Constitution (Amendment) Act bearing a sequential number.

questions.q: The 1973 case that held Parliament cannot amend the basic structure of the Constitution was:
options: Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Minerva Mills v. Union of India
questions.explanation: Kesavananda Bharati (1973) established that the amending power cannot alter the basic structure.

questions.q: The 24th Amendment of 1971 was enacted largely to clarify Parliament's power to do what?
options: Levy the Goods and Services Tax
Amend any provision, including Fundamental Rights
questions.explanation: The 24th Amendment affirmed Parliament's power under Article 368 to amend any provision, including Fundamental Rights.

questions.q: Which clause inserted by the 42nd Amendment, purporting to bar judicial review of amendments, was struck down in Minerva Mills?
questions.explanation: Minerva Mills (1980) struck down clauses (4) and (5) of Article 368 that sought to exclude judicial review.

questions.q: Before Kesavananda Bharati, the 1951 case that upheld the First Amendment and the power to amend rights was:
options: Golak Nath v. State of Punjab
Shankari Prasad v. Union of India
A.K. Gopalan v. State of Madras
questions.explanation: Shankari Prasad (1951) upheld Parliament's power to amend Fundamental Rights under Article 368.

questions.q: Which amendment added the Fundamental Duties as Part IV-A of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The 42nd Amendment of 1976 added Part IV-A containing the Fundamental Duties.

questions.q: The First Amendment of 1951 introduced which new Schedule to protect certain laws from challenge?
questions.explanation: The First Amendment added the Ninth Schedule to shield certain laws, mainly land reforms, from challenge.

questions.q: Which amendment in 2019 provided a 10 percent reservation for economically weaker sections?
questions.explanation: The 103rd Amendment of 2019 introduced reservation for economically weaker sections in education and public employment.

questions.q: Which amendment added the Eleventh and Twelfth Schedules to the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The 73rd Amendment added the Eleventh Schedule and the 74th added the Twelfth Schedule for local bodies.
